title: QLIF-CAST: Quantum Leaky-Integrate-and-Fire for Time-Series Weather Forecasting
authors: Alberto Marchisio, Aayan Ebrahim, Nouhaila Innan, Muhammad Kashif, Muhammad Shafique
abstract: Accurate and efficient time-series forecasting remains a challenging problem for both classical and quantum neural architectures, particularly in multivariate environmental settings. This work adapts the Quantum Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (QLIF) spiking neural network for time-series regression tasks, specifically short-term multivariate weather forecasting. We extend QLIF beyond classification and demonstrate its applicability to continuous-valued prediction problems.   The QLIF-CAST model encodes neuron excitation states as single-qubit quantum superpositions, driven by Rx rotation gates and T1 relaxation decay, and is embedded within a hybrid quantum-classical recurrent architecture. We conduct two distinct evaluations. First, a controlled comparison against a parameter-matched classical LIF baseline on a multivariate weather dataset shows that QLIF-CAST achieves 15.4% lower MSE and 4.4% lower MAE, demonstrating that quantum neuronal dynamics reduce prediction error over classical equivalents. Second, a cross-domain comparative analysis with state-of-the-art quantum LSTM (QLSTM) and quantum neural network (QNN) models on air quality and wind speed benchmarks reveals that QLIF-CAST converges in up to 94% less training time, occupying a distinct position in the speed-error trade-off space. Hardware verification on IBM Marrakesh (156-qubit QPU) confirms reliable circuit execution with only 1.2% average deviation from simulation.
summary: This paper introduces QLIF-CAST, a lightweight hybrid quantum neural network designed for time-series forecasting. By using shallow single-qubit circuits to model neuronal dynamics, the model significantly reduces training time compared to deeper quantum models while outperforming classical counterparts in prediction accuracy.
main_problem: The challenge of developing efficient and accurate quantum-classical hybrid architectures for multivariate time-series weather forecasting, specifically addressing the high computational cost of deep variational quantum models.
main_result: The QLIF-CAST model achieves 15.4% lower MSE than a classical LIF baseline and converges up to 94% faster than state-of-the-art QLSTM and QNN models, while maintaining hardware reliability on a 156-qubit QPU.
method: The authors use a hybrid recurrent architecture with surrogate gradient training (arctan approximation) and a vectorized circuit execution strategy to achieve a 500x speedup in simulation.
model_or_system: A hybrid quantum-classical recurrent architecture (QLIF-CAST) utilizing a depth-2, single-qubit circuit where neuron excitation states are encoded as quantum superpositions driven by Rx rotations and T1 relaxation decay.
key_observables: Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and R-squared (R2).
important_parameters: T1 relaxation constant of 10.0, spike threshold of 0.75, and 12-step context windows.
main_assumptions: The model assumes that the higher error in certain benchmarks remains within operational tolerances for practical deployment and that the shallow circuit depth is sufficient to capture necessary dynamics.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates the QLIF neuron model; Fig 2 shows the classical LIF model; Fig 7 shows training/validation loss convergence; Fig 8 and 9 show forecasting performance for air quality and wind speed respectively.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QLIF-CAST model, describes the quantum neuronal dynamics and architecture, presents a controlled comparison against classical LIF, performs a cross-domain benchmark against QLSTM/QNN, and concludes with hardware verification on IBM Marrakesh.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. QLIF neuron model. The qubit is initialized in |0⟩, rotated by Rx(ϕ) to encode the current excitation state, then rotated by Rx(θinput) to integrate the input signal. Measuring the qubit yields the updated excitation probability αnew = sin2( ϕ+θinput

Fig. 2. Classical LIF neuron model. The membrane potential U integrates synaptic input Iin and decays exponentially between spikes (Unew = βUprev + (1−β)Iin, β = e−1/τ). A spike is emitted when U exceeds the threshold, after which U resets to zero.

Fig. 3. QLSTM architecture. Each of the four classical LSTM gates (forget, input, cell, output) is replaced by a multi-qubit variational quantum circuit (VQC), with gate angles trained via parameter-shift gradient rules.
Fig. 4. LSTM-QNN architecture. A classical LSTM encoder performs temporal feature extraction, whose output is passed to a variational quantum neural network (QNN) before the regression head.

Fig. 5. Overview of the QLIF-CAST methodology. Three datasets (D1-D3) used across the two evaluation phases, with shared preprocessing steps. QLIF-CAST and Classical LIF architectures; the two pipelines are structurally identical, while only Layer 2 (the neuronal update rule) differs. Two-phase evaluation design and key results, with QPU hardware validation on IBM Marrakesh.
